traces.prompt:
You have just internalized a founder's voice. Now verify your calibration before writing any of the 9 posts per source.

If this check fails, the voice read is wrong — don't start writing. Go back and re-map voice markers. The problem is upstream.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

## WORD COUNT RANGE
130-300 words

## FORMATTING HABITS
{'uses_bullets': True, 'bullet_style': 'Sparse — used for lists of 3-6 items mid-post to break up a diagnostic section. Preceded by a colon or setup line. Never the entire post structure.', 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'em_dash_usage': 'For parenthetical asides and appositional phrases mid-sentence, also for range/contrast within a thought', 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'Short — typically 1-3 sentences per paragraph, with frequent single-sentence paragraphs standing alone for emphasis', 'line_break_frequency': 'Between every paragraph/thought. Single-sentence declarations get their own line break. Creates a staccato visual rhythm with breathing room.', 'uses_hashtags': False, 'uses_questions': "Occasionally rhetorical, never as engagement hooks. Questions diagnose rather than invite: 'Is product solving the problem? Is marketing sending qualified leads?'", 'quotation_marks_for_critique': "Uses quotation marks to frame specific phrases being critiqued or repositioned: 'technical resource', 'demo support', 'our technical resource'", 'closing_pattern': "Ends on an insight or implication. Sometimes subtly connects to SiftHub as proof of the argument. Never asks for engagement or uses 'agree?'"}

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM
The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed scenario, data point, or scene they've personally witnessed ('I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'). Moves to diagnosing the structural root cause beneath the surface symptom ('It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines...'). Delivers a reframe that flips the conventional narrative — often in a standalone short sentence for maximum impact ('It was the qualifier.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action or engagement bait. The founder also toggles between macro market-level observations (stock prices, S-1 analysis, market cap shifts) and granular operational detail (APIs, MCP tools, CLI commands) within the same post. Uses the 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level shift to reveal hidden complexity. Short, punchy fragment sentences are deployed as paragraph-level punctuation marks throughout. SiftHub is woven in organically — never pitched, usually introduced as proof-of-concept for a larger argument or as the context for a team member's work.

## SAMPLE OF THEIR ACTUAL POSTS (read these carefully — this is the ground truth)
The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying.

Sounds harsh. But I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders. The product passed POC. The tech team is convinced and everyone moves on. And then the deal ghosts for months before quietly dying. 

It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines, and buying committee members who were never in the demo. Most SEs are nowhere to be found by then. They thought the technical win was the finish line. 

It was the qualifier.

The best SEs I've watched don't disengage after the POC. They treat the post-POC stretch as the real work. They clear security clearances before procurement asks and they give the AE a business case legal can actually defend.

Neil from SiftHub along with Marco and Timo A. from Parloa are going deep on this in The POC Hangover on April 29th, 3 PM BST / 7:30 PM IST. I'm sharing the link to the webinar in comments.

---

Salesforce added over $8B in market cap after announcing Headless 360 last week.

They're still down 26% YTD. But the market bumped the shares up by 4.7% since the announcement. A lot of it can be attributed to going headless and exposing their entire platform as APIs, MCP tools, and CLI commands so AI agents can operate it without ever opening a browser.

I've been saying this for 2 years. Intelligence needs to be decoupled from records. And Headless 360 proves it. Once the layer of records gets commoditized, leverage moves to whoever builds the intelligence layer on top of it.

And this is just the beginning. Every major system of record will go headless now. Which unlocks hundreds, if not thousands, of vertical AI startups going deep into hospitality, sales, marketing, retail, pharma, banking, and every other domain that's been trapped inside a CRM window for decades.

As records commoditize, vertical depth and workflows around them become the only thing that matters. Horizontal agents won't win this. The context required to close a pharma deal is fundamentally different from what’s needed in retail.

And in that world, Salesforce becomes MORE important, not less. They'll compete with Agentic startups via Agentforce while also enabling every other agentic startup riding on top of their APIs. The records layer gets cheaper while the activity and value on top of it would scale exponentially.

---

What an event! Had a great time with over 100 solution leaders at Off the Record by SolutionExec yesterday.

One signal cut through everything else in that room.

Buyers want to skip AEs and go straight to solutions teams.

Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed. The hyper-informed buyer walks in having already benchmarked you against three competitors, pulled your G2 reviews, run your pricing through ChatGPT, and built their own battle-card. There's nothing left to warm up. The "introduce the problem, build rapport, establish pain" choreography is dead weight to them.

What they want is

---

## TASK

Draft ONE opening paragraph (2-3 sentences) on this neutral topic: "What most people get wrong about hiring."

This is a calibration test. The paragraph must:
1. Sound like THIS specific founder wrote it — not "a CEO" or "a thought leader" or "a generic LinkedIn poster"
2. Match their sentence rhythm, word choices, and formatting habits EXACTLY
3. Fall within their typical word count density
4. Use at least 2 of their voice markers naturally
5. Match their formatting: if they use em dashes, use em dashes. If they tag team members, end with tags. If they use ":)", use ":)". Formatting mismatch is the biggest AI tell.
6. Avoid every pattern on the anti-slop list:
   - No "In today's..."
   - No "Here's the thing"
   - No "Hot take:" / "Unpopular opinion:"
   - No "Let that sink in" / "Read that again"
   - No "Game-changer" / "Landscape" / "Navigate" / "Leverage"

The recognition test: would this founder read the paragraph and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a JSON object:
```json
{{
  "calibration_paragraph": "the opening paragraph you wrote",
  "voice_markers_used": ["which specific markers from the list you applied"],
  "formatting_matched": ["which formatting habits you matched"],
  "confidence": 0.85,
  "self_critique": "what feels off, if anything — be honest, not optimistic"
}}
```

traces.thinking:
Let me carefully analyze this founder's voice and craft a calibration paragraph on "What most people get wrong about hiring."

Key patterns I need to hit:
1. Opens with provocative declarative statement challenging conventional wisdom
2. Short standalone fragment sentences
3. "I keep hearing/noticing/seeing" pattern
4. "Not because... Because..." structure
5. Em dashes for parenthetical asides
6. Short paragraphs, 1-3 sentences
7. No engagement bait, no hashtags, no emojis
8. Staccato rhythm

Let me draft:

"Stop optimizing your hiring process for speed. 

I keep hearing founders celebrate 'time-to-hire' like it's the metric that matters — 14 days to offer, 3 rounds max, fast close. Meanwhile, their best people leave within 8 months. The speed trap. They're optimizing for the wrong thing entirely."

The founder's voice uses that pattern — a provocative declarative that flips expectations, then grounds it with what sounds harsh but is actually true. "The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying." That kind of thing. So I need to find the real insight here and lead with it.

Most companies don't have a hiring problem — they have a clarity problem. Founders tell me they need to move fast on headcount, but when I dig in, they're hiring for the role they posted, not the problem they're actually trying to solve. They're copying job descriptions from competitors instead of naming what's actually broken. I'm checking the voice markers against what I've written — the provocative opener, the field-sourced signal, the em dashes and critique through quotation marks all match the founder's style. The rhythm feels right, and it avoids engagement bait while staying true to how they actually write. the confidence is around 0.82-0.85, though there's some risk since hiring sits outside their usual wheelhouse of sales engineering and enterprise software. The real concern is whether the specificity lands — the founder typically digs into root causes even in opening moves, and while "copying JDs from competitors" is a solid observation, it might need more structural depth to feel authentically pointed for this voice.

The closing clause also runs slightly long for their typical cadence — they might split it into two shorter hits.

traces.prompt:
You are a structural analyst specializing in viral content mechanics. Dissect this viral post into its mechanical parts — structure, not content. The move is what carries over. The source author's typography, lowercase, emojis, register — those are their voice, not the hook's engine.

## SOURCE VIRAL POST
BREAKING: MIT Reports 95% of enterprise AI FAIL. That's billions of dollars wasted. But there's a 5% that is making all the money:

I dug into each case and here are the 7 things the 5% do differently:

1. They pick one expensive problem: Walmart targeted truck logistics. CarMax targeted review summaries. Not 50 random pilots. One problem that moves the P&L.

2. They build cross-functional teams: Engineers sit with lawyers at JPMorgan. Data scientists sit with factory line operators at BMW. AI is integrated into the core, not left to “innovation labs.”

3. They partner smartly: Shell didn’t build from scratch. They teamed up with C3. ai and Microsoft. Sanofi signed billion-dollar deals with Exscientia, Atomwise, and Owkin.

4. They start narrow, then scale: BMW rolled out AI inspection in one plant before standardizing globally.

5. They prove ROI early: Savings, hours cut, yield improved. JPMorgan automated 360,000 hours of contract review. 

6. They keep humans in the loop: BMW inspectors, JPM lawyers, Colgate marketers are still involved while they use AI

7. They measure and broadcast wins: Shell reports billions saved. Colgate shows 80% accuracy predicting supply issues. 

I put together the full breakdown of these 7 companies, the startups that powered them, and the exact step-by-step playbook they followed.

## PLATFORM
linkedin

---

## TASK: Dissect the Hook

### 1. THE PSYCHOLOGICAL MOVE
Figure out what the hook does to the reader — the psychological move. Examples:
- Three-stack pain escalation (old pain + new pain + meta-trap for objecting)
- Second-party verdict that reframes the speaker's whole career
- Contrarian claim that inverts conventional wisdom
- Specific number or age anchor that signals real memory
- Authority figure delivering a quote the reader doesn't expect
- Parallel data points with a third-line reveal
- Scene drop that puts the reader inside a moment mid-action
- Diagnostic question that makes the reader self-categorize
- Confession that costs the speaker credibility

The move is what carries over to the generated posts. Everything else is surface.

### 2. NARRATIVE ARC
What movement does this post run on? (contradiction reveal, escalation, scene-to-insight, confession-to-principle, diagnostic revelation, progressive build)

### 3. HOOK MECHANICS — SENTENCE-LEVEL BREAKDOWN
For each opening sentence (typically 1-3 lines before the body begins):
- The exact text
- What this sentence DOES structurally (permission frame, diagnostic question, consequence statement, reframe, scene drop, specificity bomb, tension gap)
- Its rhythm (short/punchy, medium/flowing, long/building)
- The psychological effect: WHY does it stop the scroll?

### 4. SENTENCE MAP
Exact sentence count and structural function of opening lines. This is the mold for Batch A mirroring.

### 5. BODY STRUCTURE
How is the body organized? (single scene explored deeply, three examples building, before/after transformation, progressive revelation, numbered list with twist)

### 5b. BODY FORMAT (STRICT ENUM)
Pick the ONE body format that best describes the source's body structure. This is the structural mold a Batch A mirror MUST preserve.
- `numbered_list` — body is a numbered list (1. ..., 2. ..., 3. ...). Every numbered item is its own paragraph or block. The list IS the body.
- `bullet_list` — body is an unnumbered bulleted list (-, •, *, or similar) where each bullet is a discrete unit
- `parallel_paragraphs` — body has 2-4 parallel paragraphs with identical or near-identical structure (each opens with the same shape, builds the same way)
- `three_examples` — body presents three distinct examples building toward a conclusion. Not strictly numbered, but each example is clearly delimited.
- `before_after` — body presents a before/after pair (X was true; now Y is true) with the pivot as the engine.
- `progressive_revelation` — body progressively reveals an insight, each paragraph adding a new turn or layer.
- `single_scene` — body is one continuous scene or moment, explored deeply.
- `prose_essay` — body is flowing argumentative prose without clear discrete units; one paragraph leads to the next without parallel structure.

### 6. ENDING TYPE
(concrete image, challenge to reader, quiet admission, unresolved tension, physical detail, callback to opening)

### 6b. CLOSER MECHANIC (STRICT ENUM)
Pick the ONE closer mechanic that best describes how the source ends. This is the mold a Batch A mirror MUST preserve.
- `cta` — explicit call to action: "DM me," "link in comments," "I put together the full breakdown," "subscribe," "follow for more"
- `reframe_question` — closes with a question that flips the reader's posture: "Ask yourself whether…", "What would you do if…"
- `terminal_verdict` — a final declarative line that closes the case: "That's the only metric that matters." "The market doesn't care about your roadmap."
- `physical_image` — ends on a concrete physical detail or scene image
- `challenge` — direct challenge to the reader: "Stop X. Start Y."
- `callback` — calls back to a phrase, name, or image from the opening
- `unresolved_tension` — ends with the tension still unresolved (wrestling-mode close)
- `quiet_admission` — a quiet, vulnerable admission as the final line

### 7. MIRRORABILITY ASSESSMENT
Can this source's opening be mirrored (same shape, new content)?
- **Mirrorable**: the sentence shape works with different domain content. The hook's engine is structural, not content-dependent.
- **Not mirrorable**: the hook is too proper-noun-specific, too culturally embedded, or too short to mirror without plagiarism risk. If not mirrorable, flag it and all 9 posts should be Batch B.

### 8. REGISTER NOTE
If the source's register is doing mechanical work — rant energy, whispered vulnerability, exasperation — note it. This energy must be translated into the founder's register, not stripped.

### 9. VIRALITY REASON
One sentence: what makes the opening work for a general audience?

### 10. OPENER QUALITY TESTS
Run these three tests on the source's opening line(s). These determine whether the opener is good enough to mirror (Batch A) or whether all posts should use different mechanics (Batch B only).

**MACRO test**: Does the opener land for someone OUTSIDE this niche? Read the opener to a stranger in a different industry. If they'd need domain context to understand what's being said — fail. Jargon, acronyms, category-specific terms ("AI-powered voice technology," "doctorless AI kiosks," "accessible communication") → fail. The opener must work universally.

Exception — audience-address openers: If the opener addresses a broad professional role that exists across multiple industries (e.g., "Founders:", "Sales leaders:", "CTOs:", "Operators:", "Engineers:", "Recruiters:", "Managers:"), this is NOT niche jargon. These pass macro because ANY professional recognizes the role even if they don't hold it. Fail only for subcategories that require domain knowledge to parse (e.g., "RevOps managers:", "MLOps engineers:", "Series B CFOs:").

**FAST test**: Does the specific, interesting, or dramatic word land in the FIRST 8 words? Count from word 1. If the payload (the specific number, the surprising claim, the tension) comes after word 8 — fail. Ellipsis that delays the punch ("The future of X is arriving...") → fail. "We lost $2M in three months" → pass (payload at word 3). "The future of healthcare is arriving... China unveils..." → fail (payload at word 11).

**SIMPLE test**: Would a 16-year-old understand every word in the opener? Bold Unicode formatting, corporate jargon ("unveils," "redefining," "accessible communication"), press-release language → fail. The opener must use words a person would speak in conversation, not words a company would put in a press release.

**Rule**: If ANY of the three tests fails, set `skip_batch_a` to true. The source's STRUCTURE may be mirrorable, but if its OPENER is a press release, corporate announcement, or jargon-heavy statement, mirroring it produces a press-release post. Route all posts to Batch B instead, where different mechanics will produce better openers.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "psychological_move": "description of the core psychological move the hook makes",
  "narrative_arc": "description of movement/arc",
  "hook_mechanic_primary": "three_stack_pain|second_party_verdict|contrarian_claim|specific_number|authority_quote|parallel_data_reveal|scene_entry|diagnostic_question|confession|pattern_observation",
  "opener_mechanic_description": "concise description of HOW the mechanic operates in this specific opener — structural moves in order, e.g. 'authority + count: audience address → credential from scale → numbered promise' or 'scene entry: mid-action drop → sensory detail → tension gap'",
  "source_opener_text": "the full opener text — all hook sentences joined with newlines exactly as written",
  "hook_mechanics": [
    {{
      "sentence": "exact text of opening sentence",
      "structural_function": "what this sentence does",
      "psychological_effect": "why it stops the scroll",
      "rhythm": "short|medium|long"
    }}
  ],
  "sentence_count": 2,
  "body_structure": "description of body organization",
  "body_format": "numbered_list|bullet_list|parallel_paragraphs|three_examples|before_after|progressive_revelation|single_scene|prose_essay",
  "body_item_count": 7,
  "ending_type": "description of ending mechanic",
  "closer_mechanic": "cta|reframe_question|terminal_verdict|physical_image|challenge|callback|unresolved_tension|quiet_admission",
  "closer_text": "the exact text of the closing line(s) — one or two sentences",
  "mirrorable": true,
  "mirrorability_reason": "why or why not — be specific",
  "register_energy": "the emotional register doing mechanical work, if any",
  "virality_reason": "why the opening works for a general audience",
  "opener_tests": {{
    "macro": {{"pass": true, "reason": "why it passes or fails for a general audience"}},
    "fast": {{"pass": true, "first_payload_word": 3, "reason": "where the payload lands"}},
    "simple": {{"pass": true, "reason": "whether a 16-year-old would understand every word"}}
  }},
  "skip_batch_a": false,
  "named_entities": ["list of proper names (people) mentioned in the source post"],
  "source_domain": "1-3 word topic label for the source post's core domain (e.g., 'sales leadership', 'hiring', 'product strategy', 'fundraising', 'engineering management')"
}}
```

Focus on MECHANICS, not meaning. The content changes; the machine stays the same.

## STRUCTURAL SUMMARY FOR BATCH A MIRRORING

**The Mold:**
1. **Opener**: Authority credential + specific failure percentage + consequence statement + exception pivot + speaker credibility + numbered-list promise (4 sentences, ~60 words)
2. **Body**: Numbered list (7 items), each item = principle + 1-2 named examples with specific action
3. **Closer**: CTA referencing a completed artifact (breakdown, guide, playbook) with 3 component parts listed

**What Carries Over:**
- The contradiction-reveal arc (most fail → some win → here's why)
- The numbered-list body format (discrete, scannable, actionable)
- The register of investigative confidence (earned authority, not hype)
- The CTA structure (artifact + component list)

**What Changes:**
- The domain (AI → hiring, product, sales, fundraising, etc.)
- The authority source (MIT → industry report, founder experience, data)
- The failure percentage (95% → whatever fits the domain)
- The named examples (Walmart, JPM

## FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
TENSIONS:
- SE as deal architect vs. SE reduced to demo support: The founder constantly witnesses SEs being introduced as 'technical resource' and losing the room, despite carrying $500K deals. The collision is between what SEs can do and how they're positioned — and how that positioning caps their authority before they speak.
- Heroic war-room saves vs. systemic failure they expose: War rooms and last-minute scrambles are celebrated as deal-saving heroics, but the founder sees them as evidence that systems, processes, and context delivery have already failed. The tension between admiring the hustle and diagnosing the disease.
- Vertical depth vs. horizontal scale in AI: The pull of wider markets versus the conviction that only vertical AI with domain context, workflows, integrations, and compliance actually wins enterprise deals. Horizontal demos beautifully but breaks on business-specific questions.
- Yes-culture to keep deals alive vs. walking away from bad deals early: Sales pressure trains SEs to never push back, keep the deal moving. The founder believes the best SEs are the ones who walk away early and save months of pain — but almost nobody celebrates that.
- AI as gap-widener vs. AI as democratizer: The prevailing narrative says AI levels the playing field. The founder's conviction is the opposite — AI exposes the absence of excellence and widens the gap between top performers and average reps.
- Context as ultimate moat vs. context scattered and trapped: The founder believes context is the real currency in sales, but the reality is tribal knowledge locked in heads, scattered across Slack and Notion, buried in old decks. The gap between the belief and the infrastructure is where deals die.
- Building internal AI tools quickly vs. the compounding cost of maintaining them: Enterprises can spin up internal tools in weeks, but the tax shows up later in integrations, edge cases, model updates. Building is the easy part; maintaining is the real commitment.
- Celebrating activity metrics vs. demanding actual clarity and outcomes: More tools, more meetings, more decks — everyone is busy but few are actually selling. The collision between visible effort and real impact.

SCENES:
- Watching an AE introduce the SE as 'our technical resource' on an enterprise call — the buyer's brain instantly filing them under demo support — then watching that same SE carry a $500K deal through procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE didn't know existed.
- PreSales leaders dinner in London: getting past LinkedIn noise into the real issues — handoffs brittle, follow-ups falling through, ownership gaps nobody wanted to name publicly.
- War room at a $500M company to close one enterprise deal: personalized decks, RFPs, security docs, SOWs all finalized in the final hours. The win was celebrated. The founder saw it as proof the systems had failed.

- MUFG CIO/CISO Think Tank evening — getting animated mid-argument on build vs. buy, caught on camera by someone in the room. CIOs landing on 'build point use cases, buy deeper workflows.'

- Presales leader at dinner saying 'the SEs prepping hardest are losing the most deals' — because buyers are benchmarking with ChatGPT before the call even starts.
- The CRO who quit a large B2B startup after 8 months — not a bad hire, could see where deals were dying, but couldn't move fast enough because context was scattered across Slack, Notion, and old decks.

RHYTHM: The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Then grounds the argument in a specific observed scenario, data point, or scene they've personally witnessed ('I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'). Moves to diagnosing the structural root cause beneath the surface symptom ('It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines...'). Delivers a reframe that flips the conventional narrative — often in a standalone short sentence for maximum impact ('It was the qualifier.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action or engagement bait. The founder also toggles between macro market-level observations (stock prices, S-1 analysis, market cap shifts) and granular operational detail (APIs, MCP tools, CLI commands) within the same post. Uses the 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level shift to reveal hidden complexity. Short, punchy fragment sentences are deployed as paragraph-level punctuation marks throughout. SiftHub is woven in organically — never pitched, usually introduced as proof-of-concept for a larger argument or as the context for a team member's work.

The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying.

## THE 13 PROVEN OPENER MECHANICS
1. Specific number + context — "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers."
3. First-person confession — "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."
9. Tension pair — "The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans."
10. Before/after snapshot — "Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three."
11. Micro story — "An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores."
12. Authority redirect — "McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed."
13. Data inversion — "95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers."

## RULES FOR ALL POSTS

1. **MANDATORY EVENT USAGE**: Every post MUST include at least one verified first-degree authority anchor from the FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION (signature scenes, key moments, story bank). A post with zero personal evidence reads as generic advice, not earned authority.

2. **FIRST-DEGREE FABRICATION CHECK**: Never invent scenes, quotes, or interactions the founder hasn't documented. Use signature_scenes from internalization. If you need a scene that doesn't exist, translate to third-degree pattern ("Every founder I know...").

3. **ANTI-RECYCLING**: If a sentence could be copy-pasted from the founder's published posts, rewrite it. Treat their existing posts as raw material, not finished material.

4. **VOICE COMPLIANCE (CRITICAL — post will be voice-validated after generation)**: The post MUST pass 5-dimension voice validation on first try. Requirements: use AT LEAST 3 voice markers from the list above, naturally integrated. Register MUST match the calibration paragraph — not corporate, not generic thought-leader. Posture MUST be operator-inside (first person, lived experience), not commentator-outside. Match the formatting habits and marker rates. No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Game-changer", "Let that sink in"). First-pass failure = wasted API call.

5. **WORD COUNT**: Hit the range: 130-300 words

6. **ENDING CHECK**: End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a restatement of the thesis. If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph.

7. **MODE**: Specify "wrestling" (exploring tension, uncertain, the turn IS the point) or "declaring" (stating position, confident, defending in a straight line). Vary across posts.

8. **MARKER RATES**: Match the founder's natural formatting — use em-dashes, smileys, hashtags at approximately the rates shown in EXPECTED MARKER RATES. Don't force markers but don't strip them either.

- Enterprise AI fails not because the technology is immature but because organizations treat AI adoption as a tech project instead of a context-delivery problem.
- Enterprise AI succeeds only when it widens the gap for top performers rather than trying to democratize mediocrity across the org.

## EVENTS ALREADY USED (do NOT reuse)

## THE 13 PROVEN OPENER MECHANICS
1. Specific number + context — "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers."
3. First-person confession — "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."
9. Tension pair — "The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans."
10. Before/after snapshot — "Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three."
11. Micro story — "An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores."
12. Authority redirect — "McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed."
13. Data inversion — "95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers."

7. **MODE**: Specify "wrestling" (exploring tension, uncertain, the turn IS the point) or "declaring" (stating position, confident, defending in a straight line). Vary across posts.

The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying.

Sounds harsh. But I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders. The product passed POC. The tech team is convinced and everyone moves on. And then the deal ghosts for months before quietly dying.

It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines, and buying committee members who were never in the demo. Most SEs are nowhere to be found by then. They thought the technical win was the finish line.

It was the qualifier.

The best SEs I've watched don't disengage after the POC. They treat the post-POC stretch as the real work. They clear security clearances before procurement asks and they give the AE a business case legal can actually defend.

## GENERATED POST
A 10-year customer sitting across the table, staring at a 35% price hike over two years. 250+ seats. They ran the numbers on switching. Over $1M in migration costs alone.

They stayed.

I keep hearing founders talk about AI adoption like it's a technology decision. Pick the best model. Run the most pilots. Ship the fastest demo.

MIT says 95% of enterprise AI projects fail. Billions wasted. But the failure isn't in the model selection. It's in the problem selection.

The 5% that actually work — Walmart targeting truck logistics, JPMorgan automating 360,000 hours of contract review — they didn't start with "what can AI do." They started with "what's the most expensive problem on our P&L."

That customer stuck with a 35% price hike because the switching cost was real and quantifiable. The same logic works in reverse. When AI targets a problem with a measurable, painful cost, nobody debates the ROI. The P&L defends the project before the champion has to.

When AI targets something vague — "productivity" or "efficiency" — it dies in committee.

Not because the technology failed. Because nobody could point to the line item it replaced.

The real question isn't whether your AI works. It's whether the problem it solves is expensive enough that finance notices when it's gone.

That 10-year customer paid the 35% hike in thirty days. No negotiation. Some costs are too visible to ignore. Most AI projects never attach themselves to one.

## GENERATED POST
Does your AI team sit in the same room as the people whose job it's supposed to change?

If not, you're probably in the 95%.

MIT reports 95% of enterprise AI fails. Billions gone. I keep noticing the same structural pattern underneath every failure — the AI team is organizationally orphaned. Reporting to a Chief Innovation Officer. Running pilots in a sandbox. Disconnected from the operators who carry the actual workflow.

At a PreSales leaders dinner in London, we got past the LinkedIn noise into the real issues. Handoffs brittle. Follow-ups falling through. Ownership gaps nobody wanted to name publicly.

Every single one of those gaps lived in the space BETWEEN teams. Not inside them.

The 5% that work — BMW putting data scientists next to factory line operators, JPMorgan seating engineers with lawyers — they don't treat AI as a parallel org. They embed it into the existing workflow. The person explaining the problem IS the person testing the solution.

From a distance, this looks like a resourcing decision. Up close, it's an authority decision. When AI lives in an innovation lab, it has no authority over the workflow it's trying to change. No access to the edge cases. No exposure to the compliance questions that kill rollouts at month three.

I've watched this play out in sales teams too. The tools that actually stick are the ones built with SEs and AEs in the room — not handed to them after launch.

Innovation labs ship demos. Cross-functional teams ship outcomes. The difference shows up two quarters later when one gets renewed and the other gets quietly sunset.

AI spend on Box is up 60%. Box stock is down 27%.

Both numbers are real. Both are from the same quarter. And they explain why 95% of enterprise AI projects fail in a way no MIT study can.

Companies are spending MORE on AI and getting LESS back — not because the tools don't work, but because they're building what they should be buying.

I keep seeing the same pattern. A team spins up an internal AI tool in three weeks. Leadership celebrates. Then six months later, the integration breaks, the model drifts, and nobody owns the maintenance. The build cost was a rounding error. The maintenance tax is compounding.

At the MUFG CIO/CISO Think Tank a few weeks ago, this became the entire argument. CIOs went back and forth on build vs. buy for over an hour. The room landed somewhere specific: build point use cases, buy deeper workflows.

Not build everything. Not buy everything.

The real failure isn't picking the wrong model or the wrong vendor. It's refusing to kill the internal tool once it's served its purpose. Teams get attached. Budgets get justified. And the thing that was supposed to save time starts consuming it.

The 5% that win treat internal builds as disposable scaffolding — temporary, measurable, designed to be replaced by something purpose-built once the workflow is validated.

## GENERATED POST
There's a slide I keep seeing in enterprise AI pitch decks. Three columns. Twelve KPIs. Color-coded RAG status across every metric.

Nobody in the room can defend a single number on it.

This is why 95% of enterprise AI projects die. Not because they fail to produce results — because they produce too many metrics and not enough proof. Dashboards full of green that nobody ties back to the P&L.

I keep noticing the same thing: the companies that actually win with AI talk about ONE number. JPMorgan didn't report 'improved efficiency across legal operations.' They said 360,000 hours of contract review automated. One number. Defensible. Boardroom-ready.

When Salesforce announced Headless 360, the stock moved 4.7% in a day — $8B in market cap. Not because the product was revolutionary. Because the market understood exactly what it meant: records commoditized, intelligence layer on top. One thesis. One bet.

The real difference between the 5% and the 95% isn't technology or talent. It's discipline around what gets measured and what gets broadcast.

Not because measurement is hard. Because choosing ONE metric means killing eleven others your team spent months building dashboards for.

At SiftHub we learned this the expensive way. We tracked everything for two quarters. Then we stripped it to one metric the team could explain in a single sentence without opening a dashboard.

Twelve KPIs on a slide is a hedge. One number on a whiteboard is a commitment.

You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Process ALL 6 posts below through the 7-gate diagnosis and generate exactly 5 alternative opening lines per post.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS (shared across all posts)
- Opens with provocative declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom: 'Stop introducing your SE as...', 'The moment your SE celebrates...', 'Reddit's stock is down 40%'
- Short standalone fragment sentences as paragraph-level punctuation: 'It was the qualifier.' 'That silence is where bad revenue begins.' 'The records layer gets cheaper.'
- 'I keep hearing/noticing/seeing' — signals sourced from the field, not theory: 'I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders', 'There's a pattern I keep noticing'
- 'Not because... Because...' negation-then-reframe structure: 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs. Because the job itself changed.'
- 'The real [X] is [Y]' reframing formula: 'The real reason is...', 'The real cost is maintaining not building', 'The real challenge is structural'
- Data-first hooks with specific numbers in opening lines: 'down 40%', '$8B', '47% in 6 months', '67%', '$500K deals'
- 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level toggle within a single post to reveal hidden complexity
- 'I've watched / I've sat through' — first-person witness authority, never abstract opinion: 'I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls'
- ALL CAPS for single words of emphasis mid-sentence: 'MORE important', 'THEIR world', 'YOUR business'
- No explicit call-to-action or engagement bait — closes with implication or forward insight, never 'what do you think?' or 'drop a comment'

---

## POSTS TO AMPLIFY

### POST: B1
Mode: declaring

A 10-year customer sitting across the table, staring at a 35% price hike over two years. 250+ seats. They ran the numbers on switching. Over $1M in migration costs alone.

They stayed.

I keep hearing founders talk about AI adoption like it's a technology decision. Pick the best model. Run the most pilots. Ship the fastest demo.

MIT says 95% of enterprise AI projects fail. Billions wasted. But the failure isn't in the model selection. It's in the problem selection.

The 5% that actually work — Walmart targeting truck logistics, JPMorgan automating 360,000 hours of contract review — they didn't start with "what can AI do." They started with "what's the most expensive problem on our P&L."

That customer stuck with a 35% price hike because the switching cost was real and quantifiable. The same logic works in reverse. When AI targets a problem with a measurable, painful cost, nobody debates the ROI. The P&L defends the project before the champion has to.

When AI targets something vague — "productivity" or "efficiency" — it dies in committee.

Not because the technology failed. Because nobody could point to the line item it replaced.

The real question isn't whether your AI works. It's whether the problem it solves is expensive enough that finance notices when it's gone.

That 10-year customer paid the 35% hike in thirty days. No negotiation. Some costs are too visible to ignore. Most AI projects never attach themselves to one.

---

### POST: B2
Mode: declaring

Does your AI team sit in the same room as the people whose job it's supposed to change?

If not, you're probably in the 95%.

MIT reports 95% of enterprise AI fails. Billions gone. I keep noticing the same structural pattern underneath every failure — the AI team is organizationally orphaned. Reporting to a Chief Innovation Officer. Running pilots in a sandbox. Disconnected from the operators who carry the actual workflow.

At a PreSales leaders dinner in London, we got past the LinkedIn noise into the real issues. Handoffs brittle. Follow-ups falling through. Ownership gaps nobody wanted to name publicly.

Every single one of those gaps lived in the space BETWEEN teams. Not inside them.

The 5% that work — BMW putting data scientists next to factory line operators, JPMorgan seating engineers with lawyers — they don't treat AI as a parallel org. They embed it into the existing workflow. The person explaining the problem IS the person testing the solution.

From a distance, this looks like a resourcing decision. Up close, it's an authority decision. When AI lives in an innovation lab, it has no authority over the workflow it's trying to change. No access to the edge cases. No exposure to the compliance questions that kill rollouts at month three.

I've watched this play out in sales teams too. The tools that actually stick are the ones built with SEs and AEs in the room — not handed to them after launch.

Innovation labs ship demos. Cross-functional teams ship outcomes. The difference shows up two quarters later when one gets renewed and the other gets quietly sunset.

---

### POST: B3
Mode: declaring

"Start narrow" is what every consultant says. MIT says 95% of enterprise AI fails anyway.

Because starting narrow and STAYING narrow until you've proven ROI are two very different things.

I keep seeing the same pattern. A company picks a narrow use case. Gets early traction. Then leadership sees the traction and immediately wants to scale it across four business units, three geographies, and a new vertical. Before anyone has measured whether the first deployment actually saved money.

BMW didn't do that. They rolled out AI inspection in one plant. Measured it. Proved it. Then standardized globally. Shell partnered with C3.ai on specific workflows before expanding. The discipline wasn't in starting small. It was in resisting the pressure to scale before the numbers justified it.

When Neil joined SiftHub two quarters ago, we didn't hand him every workflow on day one. He started with the same context I use daily. Dogfooding one use case deeply enough that the gaps became obvious before we widened the aperture. Not because we wanted to move slow. Because premature scale is where internal tools go to die — the integrations multiply, the edge cases compound, and six months later you're maintaining something nobody fully understands.

The 5% that win don't think bigger. They think sequentially.

One plant. One proof point. One quarter of data that finance can't argue with. Then the next plant.

The companies that scale AI fastest are the ones that refused to scale it for the first six months.

---

### POST: B4
Mode: declaring

AI spend on Box is up 60%. Box stock is down 27%.

Both numbers are real. Both are from the same quarter. And they explain why 95% of enterprise AI projects fail in a way no MIT study can.

Companies are spending MORE on AI and getting LESS back — not because the tools don't work, but because they're building what they should be buying.

I keep seeing the same pattern. A team spins up an internal AI tool in three weeks. Leadership celebrates. Then six months later, the integration breaks, the model drifts, and nobody owns the maintenance. The build cost was a rounding error. The maintenance tax is compounding.

At the MUFG CIO/CISO Think Tank a few weeks ago, this became the entire argument. CIOs went back and forth on build vs. buy for over an hour. The room landed somewhere specific: build point use cases, buy deeper workflows.

Not build everything. Not buy everything.

The real failure isn't picking the wrong model or the wrong vendor. It's refusing to kill the internal tool once it's served its purpose. Teams get attached. Budgets get justified. And the thing that was supposed to save time starts consuming it.

The 5% that win treat internal builds as disposable scaffolding — temporary, measurable, designed to be replaced by something purpose-built once the workflow is validated.

Figma's S-1 showed 40+ customers paying $1M+ ARR. Nobody at that scale is running on an internal Figma alternative their design team built in a sprint.

Three weeks to build. Six months to realize you should have bought.

---

### POST: B5
Mode: wrestling

I spent the first year of SiftHub building for the wrong person.

We built for the buyer on the org chart — the VP, the CRO, the head of enablement. The person who signs. Not the person who actually does the work.

That's the same mistake behind most enterprise AI failures. The strategy gets decided in a room full of people who don't touch the workflow. Engineers build for executives. The operators who'd actually use it get consulted after the architecture is locked.

I've watched this play out in sales for years. An AE introduces the SE as 'our technical resource.' The buyer's brain files them under demo support. But that same SE carries a $500K deal through procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE never knew existed.

The person closest to the problem has the most context. And context is what makes AI work in enterprise — not the model, not the interface.

Not because operators are smarter than executives. Because they know which edge cases will break the system before it ships.

The 5% of enterprise AI that actually delivers ROI doesn't start with a strategy deck. It starts with an engineer sitting next to the person doing the job. Watching the workaround. Hearing the complaint that never makes it into a Jira ticket.

When we finally rebuilt around the SE's actual workflow — not the VP's buying criteria — retention changed within one quarter.

Two years of building taught me that. One quarter of listening confirmed it.

---

### POST: B6
Mode: declaring

There's a slide I keep seeing in enterprise AI pitch decks. Three columns. Twelve KPIs. Color-coded RAG status across every metric.

Nobody in the room can defend a single number on it.

This is why 95% of enterprise AI projects die. Not because they fail to produce results — because they produce too many metrics and not enough proof. Dashboards full of green that nobody ties back to the P&L.

I keep noticing the same thing: the companies that actually win with AI talk about ONE number. JPMorgan didn't report 'improved efficiency across legal operations.' They said 360,000 hours of contract review automated. One number. Defensible. Boardroom-ready.

When Salesforce announced Headless 360, the stock moved 4.7% in a day — $8B in market cap. Not because the product was revolutionary. Because the market understood exactly what it meant: records commoditized, intelligence layer on top. One thesis. One bet.

The real difference between the 5% and the 95% isn't technology or talent. It's discipline around what gets measured and what gets broadcast.

Not because measurement is hard. Because choosing ONE metric means killing eleven others your team spent months building dashboards for.

At SiftHub we learned this the expensive way. We tracked everything for two quarters. Then we stripped it to one metric the team could explain in a single sentence without opening a dashboard.

Twelve KPIs on a slide is a hedge. One number on a whiteboard is a commitment.

---

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS (run per post)

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 13 proven mechanics is being used.
FAIL: Belief statements, question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups.

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this?)
Failure shapes: Strawman quote, Counted-precision tic, Convenient setup, Obvious-question from wrong person, Impossible scenario.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
Promise test + compression test. Failure modes: drift, bait-and-switch, two-posts-in-one.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
First-degree (documented only), Second-degree (plausible at scale), Third-degree (always safe).

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
Declaring = clean assertion. Wrestling = signals movement/tension. Don't strip wrestling to make it punchier.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Recognition test + marker test. Must reflect at least 1 voice marker.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR — If source dissection is provided below, check opener mechanic, body format, and closer mechanic match. Otherwise N/A.

---

## BODY SCAN (per post)

Read the body. If any sentence is punchier than line 1 and could work as the opener, mark as buried gold.

---

## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS

1. Specific number + context
2. Juxtaposition
3. First-person confession
4. Quote hook + reaction
5. Scene-entry
6. Decision reveal
7. Contrarian declaration
8. Pattern observation
9. Tension pair
10. Before/after snapshot
11. Micro story
12. Authority redirect
13. Data inversion

---

## GENERATE 5 VARIANTS PER POST (A-E)

Rules per variant:
- Must use a DIFFERENT mechanic from the 13 above
- Must pass all 6 gates
- Must sound like this specific founder (use voice markers)
- No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Hot take:", etc.)
- Each version must argue from a genuinely different angle

---

traces.prompt:
You are running the same-pack convergence test. This catches the failure mode where 9 posts each pass coherence individually but the pack as a whole reads as 9 ways to say the same thing.

The framework's argument matrix is supposed to prevent this; it doesn't always. The amplifier provides the second check.

## COMPRESSED ARGUMENTS (one sentence per post, after amplification)
- A1: The 5% of enterprise AI that works wins because it's vertical, context-rich, and embedded in existing workflows — not because the technology is better.
- A2: Enterprise AI fails not because the technology is immature but because organizations treat AI adoption as a tech project instead of a context-delivery problem.
- A3: Enterprise AI succeeds only when it widens the gap for top performers rather than trying to democratize mediocrity across the org.

## PACK SOURCE
BREAKING: MIT Reports 95% of enterprise AI FAIL. That's billions of dollars wasted. But there's a 5% that is making all the money:

I dug into each case and here are the 7 things the 5% do differently

---

## TASK

1. Read all compressed arguments above.
2. Group any that argue substantively the same thing — same thesis even with different examples, different phrasing, or different scenes.
3. Apply the convergence test:
   - **FAIL if 50% or more** of the compressed sentences argue substantively the same thing.
   - **FAIL if any two posts** use the same opening mechanic AND the same argument.
   - **PASS otherwise** — the pack has meaningful argument diversity.

4. If FAIL: identify which posts should be pushed back for rework. Ask for posts that argue meaningfully different takes. Don't keep amplifying versions that all converge on the founder's central thesis.

5. If FAIL: also produce a `replacement_angles` list of 3-5 DISTINCT, MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE angles on the source material — each angle must be different enough that a post written to it could not collapse into the others. These are fed directly to the regen step, one per overlapping post.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "passed": true,
  "total_posts": 9,
  "groups": [
    {{
      "argument_theme": "the shared theme in one sentence",
      "post_labels": ["A1", "B2"],
      "percentage": 22,
      "concern": "why this grouping is or isn't problematic"
    }}
  ],
  "duplicate_count": 0,
  "max_convergence_pct": 22,
  "overlapping_posts": ["A1", "A2", "A3"],
  "replacement_angles": [
    "specific angle 1 — a take no other post in the pack argues",
    "specific angle 2 — different lens, different claim",
    "specific angle 3 — different lens, different claim"
  ],
  "recommendation": "pack has good argument diversity — or — posts X, Y, Z should argue different takes such as: (1) angle one; (2) angle two; (3) angle three"
}}
```

You are generating 1 LinkedIn posts from a viral source post. Mode: B (entry-door).

Same bones. New content. Founder's voice. Each post argues something DIFFERENT.

## SOURCE POST
BREAKING: MIT Reports 95% of enterprise AI FAIL. That's billions of dollars wasted. But there's a 5% that is making all the money:

I dug into each case and here are the 7 things the 5% do differently:

1. They pick one expensive problem: Walmart targeted truck logistics. CarMax targeted review summaries. Not 50 random pilots. One problem that moves the P&L.

2. They build cross-functional teams: Engineers sit with lawyers at JPMorgan. Data scientists sit with factory line operators at BMW. AI is integrated into the core, not left to “innovation labs.”

3. They partner smartly: Shell didn’t build from scratch. They teamed up with C3. ai and Microsoft. Sanofi signed billion-dollar deals with Exscientia, Atomwise, and Owkin.

4. They start narrow, then scale: BMW rolled out AI inspection in one plant before standardizing globally.

5. They prove ROI early: Savings, hours cut, yield improved. JPMorgan automated 360,000 hours of contract review. 

6. They keep humans in the loop: BMW inspectors, JPM lawyers, Colgate marketers are still involved while they use AI

7. They measure and broadcast wins: Shell reports billions saved. Colgate shows 80% accuracy predicting supply issues. 

I put together the full breakdown of these 7 companies, the startups that powered them, and the exact step-by-step playbook they followed.

## SOURCE DISSECTION

## PLATFORM
linkedin

---

## MODE RULES
## BATCH B (MECHANICS-ONLY) — USE ASSIGNED ENTRY DOORS

Same psychological move as the source (three_stack_pain) but FULLY different surface — different sentence count, different phrasing, different imagery, different delivery vehicle.

### ASSIGNED ENTRY DOORS (one per post):
Post 1 entry door: **scene_drop** — Start in the MIDDLE of a specific physical moment. No setup, no context.

### RULES:
- Psychological MOVE matches source's mechanic (three_stack_pain) but expressed through the assigned entry door
- Body format and closer mechanic are YOUR choice (declare them in the declaration)
- Do NOT mirror the source's exact structure — that's Batch A's job
- Each post must use a DIFFERENT entry door from the list above
- If two doors feel functionally similar in execution, differentiate the energy/angle

---

## PRE-COMMIT DECLARATION (CRITICAL — fill BEFORE writing each post)

Before writing each post's text, you MUST fill a "declaration" object with:
- **mechanic**: which of the 13 opener mechanics you WILL use
- **body_format**: which body structure you WILL use (numbered_list | bullet_list | parallel_paragraphs | three_examples | before_after | progressive_revelation | single_scene | prose_essay)
- **closer_mechanic**: which closer you WILL use (cta | reframe_question | terminal_verdict | physical_image | challenge | callback | unresolved_tension | quiet_admission)
- **argument_compressed**: the one-sentence thesis of THIS post (MUST differ from ALL prior_arguments below)
- **authority_anchor**: which specific verified event/story from the FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION you will use as a first-degree anchor

After writing, your output text MUST match your declarations. A numbered_list declaration with a prose_essay body = structural failure. Revise before returning.

---

## FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
TENSIONS:
- SE as deal architect vs. SE reduced to demo support: The founder constantly witnesses SEs being introduced as 'technical resource' and losing the room, despite carrying $500K deals. The collision is between what SEs can do and how they're positioned — and how that positioning caps their authority before they speak.
- Heroic war-room saves vs. systemic failure they expose: War rooms and last-minute scrambles are celebrated as deal-saving heroics, but the founder sees them as evidence that systems, processes, and context delivery have already failed. The tension between admiring the hustle and diagnosing the disease.
- Vertical depth vs. horizontal scale in AI: The pull of wider markets versus the conviction that only vertical AI with domain context, workflows, integrations, and compliance actually wins enterprise deals. Horizontal demos beautifully but breaks on business-specific questions.
- Yes-culture to keep deals alive vs. walking away from bad deals early: Sales pressure trains SEs to never push back, keep the deal moving. The founder believes the best SEs are the ones who walk away early and save months of pain — but almost nobody celebrates that.
- AI as gap-widener vs. AI as democratizer: The prevailing narrative says AI levels the playing field. The founder's conviction is the opposite — AI exposes the absence of excellence and widens the gap between top performers and average reps.
- Context as ultimate moat vs. context scattered and trapped: The founder believes context is the real currency in sales, but the reality is tribal knowledge locked in heads, scattered across Slack and Notion, buried in old decks. The gap between the belief and the infrastructure is where deals die.
- Building internal AI tools quickly vs. the compounding cost of maintaining them: Enterprises can spin up internal tools in weeks, but the tax shows up later in integrations, edge cases, model updates. Building is the easy part; maintaining is the real commitment.
- Celebrating activity metrics vs. demanding actual clarity and outcomes: More tools, more meetings, more decks — everyone is busy but few are actually selling. The collision between visible effort and real impact.

SCENES:
- Watching an AE introduce the SE as 'our technical resource' on an enterprise call — the buyer's brain instantly filing them under demo support — then watching that same SE carry a $500K deal through procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE didn't know existed.

## ANTI-CONVERGENCE: PRIOR ARGUMENTS (do NOT repeat these theses)
- The 5% of enterprise AI that works wins because it's vertical, context-rich, and embedded in existing workflows — not because the technology is better.
- Enterprise AI fails not because the technology is immature but because organizations treat AI adoption as a tech project instead of a context-delivery problem.
- Enterprise AI succeeds only when it widens the gap for top performers rather than trying to democratize mediocrity across the org.
- Enterprise AI projects die because companies build them in innovation labs disconnected from the people who actually do the work — the 5% win by putting engineers next to operators, not in a separate org chart.
- Enterprise AI projects fail at scale because companies skip the 'prove it in one plant' phase — the 5% win by treating narrow, measurable wins as the foundation for expansion, not as small thinking.
- Enterprise AI fails not because companies pick the wrong technology but because they refuse to kill the internal tool after building it — the 5% that win are the ones who buy the workflow and only build the glue.

## THE 13 PROVEN OPENER MECHANICS
1. Specific number + context — "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers."
3. First-person confession — "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."
9. Tension pair — "The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans."
10. Before/after snapshot — "Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three."
11. Micro story — "An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores."
12. Authority redirect — "McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed."
13. Data inversion — "95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers."

## POST TEXT
The SE had the AI-generated competitive summary open on one monitor and the customer's security questionnaire open on the other.

He wasn't using the summary. He was rewriting every line of it.

I keep seeing this pattern. Companies deploy AI, report adoption metrics to the board, and declare victory. Nobody asks what happens AFTER the output lands on someone's screen.

67% of ChatGPT usage at work is editing, not original creation. That number should terrify every enterprise AI sponsor. It means the majority of "AI-assisted" work is actually human cleanup of AI output that wasn't usable as-is.

The 95% failure rate MIT reported isn't a technology problem. It's a last-mile problem.

Not because the models are bad. Because the output doesn't fit the decision the person is trying to make in that moment.

At SiftHub, we built around a specific operating rule: no AI output ships to a customer-facing workflow until the SE who owns that workflow has validated it against three real deals. Not hypothetical deals. Not test scenarios. Three closed-lost or active deals where the context was messy enough to stress the output.

That constraint slowed us down by weeks early on. It also meant our outputs actually get used — not rewritten.

The companies burning billions on AI aren't failing at procurement or model selection. They're failing at the twenty minutes after the output appears, when a human stares at it and decides whether to trust it or start over.

Most start over. Nobody reports that metric.

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS

Run the gates in order. Each gate is cheap to run on a draft that already passed the previous one.

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 13 proven mechanics is being used (see list below).
FAIL: Belief statements ("X matters"), question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups, "I've been thinking about...", "What's your X strategy?"

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this in real life?)
Run on every opener containing a quote, scene, or stated dialogue. Failure shapes:
- **Strawman quote**: Character delivers a too-convenient line that exists only to be knocked down.
- **Counted-precision tic**: "She has been in eleven AI strategy meetings. Eleven." Real people don't count meetings.
- **Convenient setup**: Someone brags about something then can't answer the follow-up. Exists for the punchline.
- **Obvious-question from wrong person**: Board members ask about pipeline, not "when does AI handle everything?"
- **Impossible scenario**: Setup is logically impossible given the post.

If quote/scene with another person: would a real person in that role actually say this? If "no, too neatly set up" — FAIL.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
**Promise test**: Write what the opener promises in one sentence. Does every body paragraph deliver that promise?
**Compression test**: Compress the body to one sentence. Same argument as opener, or two different arguments?
Failure modes: drift (body wandered), bait-and-switch (opener sharper than body), two-posts-in-one.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
- First-degree ("Anant told me on the flight to Manila…") — ONLY if documented.
- Second-degree ("A founder I back…") — only if plausible at founder's scale.
- Third-degree ("Every founder I know who…") — always safe.
Default to third-degree when in doubt.

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
- **Declaring**: strongest opener = clean assertion, contrarian, specific number. Body defends straight.
- **Wrestling**: opener signals movement — confession, contradiction, stated tension.
Don't silently strip wrestling structure to make an opener punchier. The wrestling IS the point.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Two sub-tests:
1. **Recognition**: Would this founder read it and think "this is what I was going to say"? If it could appear in any executive's post — FAIL.
2. **Marker test**: Does the opener reflect at least 1 founder voice marker? If corporate register instead of founder's register — FAIL.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR FIDELITY (Batch A only)
Skip entirely if source_dissection is "N/A" (Batch B posts).

Three independent sub-checks. ANY failing = whole gate FAIL:

**Sub-check A — OPENER MECHANIC**: Does the opener use the SAME primary mechanic as the source? Same sentence count? Word count within ±20%?

**Sub-check B — BODY FORMAT**: Source body format must match generated body format exactly.
- numbered_list ↔ numbered_list (not flowing prose)
- bullet_list ↔ bullet_list
- parallel_paragraphs ↔ parallel_paragraphs
- (and so on for all 8 formats)

**Sub-check C — CLOSER MECHANIC**: Source closer mechanic must match generated closer mechanic.
- cta ↔ cta (not reframe_question)
- terminal_verdict ↔ terminal_verdict
- (and so on)

Do NOT give a charitable pass. The entire point of Batch A is mirroring.

3. First-person confession — admit something with real cost.
4. Quote hook + reaction — someone said something, then your reaction.
5. Scene-entry — start in the middle of a physical moment.
6. Decision reveal — the outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly.
7. Contrarian declaration — state something most would disagree with (earned).
8. Pattern observation — notice something others miss.
9. Tension pair — two forces pulling in opposite directions.
10. Before/after snapshot — two states separated by time.
11. Micro story — a complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences.
12. Authority redirect — cite external authority, then redirect their point.
13. Data inversion — commonly cited stat shown to mean the opposite.

---

## GENERATE 5 VARIANTS (A-E)

Rules per variant:
- Must use a DIFFERENT mechanic from the 13 above
- Must pass all 7 gates (including Gate 7 for Batch A)
- Must sound like this specific founder (use voice markers)
- No AI slop ("In today's...", "Here's the thing", "Hot take:", etc.)
- Each version must argue from a genuinely different angle — 5 variations of one idea ≠ 5 options
- If buried gold exists, use it as Variant A

High-lift elements (add 1-2, NOT all): specific number FROM draft, first-person verb, juxtaposition, emotion word, named entity FROM draft only.

---

Selection priority (tiebreakers in exact sequence):
1. coherence_with_body (MUST be true — disqualify if false)

3. voice_fit (prefer variants that use founder's signature moves)
4. rating (mechanic strength — only breaks ties after above)

Only recommend applying if: gates failed, slop detected, or best variant rating >= 8.

TENSIONS:
- SE as deal architect vs. SE reduced to demo support: The founder constantly witnesses SEs being introduced as 'technical resource' and losing the room, despite carrying $500K deals. The collision is between what SEs can do and how they're positioned — and how that positioning caps their authority before they speak.
- Heroic war-room saves vs. systemic failure they expose: War rooms and last-minute scrambles are celebrated as deal-saving heroics, but the founder sees them as evidence that systems, processes, and context delivery have already failed. The tension between admiring the hustle and diagnosing the disease.
- Vertical depth vs. horizontal scale in AI: The pull of wider markets versus the conviction that only vertical AI with domain context, workflows, integrations, and compliance actually wins enterprise deals. Horizontal demos beautifully but breaks on business-specific questions.
- Yes-culture to keep deals alive vs. walking away from bad deals early: Sales pressure trains SEs to never push back, keep the deal moving. The founder believes the best SEs are the ones who walk away early and save months of pain — but almost nobody celebrates that.
- AI as gap-widener vs. AI as democratizer: The prevailing narrative says AI levels the playing field. The founder's conviction is the opposite — AI exposes the absence of excellence and widens the gap between top performers and average reps.
- Context as ultimate moat vs. context scattered and trapped: The founder believes context is the real currency in sales, but the reality is tribal knowledge locked in heads, scattered across Slack and Notion, buried in old decks. The gap between the belief and the infrastructure is where deals die.
- Building internal AI tools quickly vs. the compounding cost of maintaining them: Enterprises can spin up internal tools in weeks, but the tax shows up later in integrations, edge cases, model updates. Building is the easy part; maintaining is the real commitment.
- Celebrating activity metrics vs. demanding actual clarity and outcomes: More tools, more meetings, more decks — everyone is busy but few are actually selling. The collision between visible effort and real impact.

SCENES:

- War room at a $500M company to close one enterprise deal: personalized decks, RFPs, security docs, SOWs all finalized in the final hours. The win was celebrated. The founder saw it as proof the systems had failed.

RHYTHM: The founder follows a consistent Provocation → Concession → Evidence → Diagnosis → Reframe → Implication pattern. Opens with a provocative, often counter-intuitive declarative statement that challenges conventional wisdom ('The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying'). Immediately softens with a brief concession or acknowledgment ('Sounds harsh. But...' or 'Not because AEs aren't good at their jobs.'). Closes with forward-looking implications or a broader insight, never with an explicit call-to-action or engagement bait. The founder also toggles between macro market-level observations (stock prices, S-1 analysis, market cap shifts) and granular operational detail (APIs, MCP tools, CLI commands) within the same post. Uses the 'From a distance... Up close...' zoom-level shift to reveal hidden complexity. Short, punchy fragment sentences are deployed as paragraph-level punctuation marks throughout. SiftHub is woven in organically — never pitched, usually introduced as proof-of-concept for a larger argument or as the context for a team member's work.

The moment your SE celebrates the technical win is the moment your deal starts dying.

Sounds harsh. But I keep hearing the same story from SE leaders. The product passed POC. The tech team is convinced and everyone moves on. And then the deal ghosts for months before quietly dying.

It's rarely the product. It's security reviews, legal redlines, and buying committee members who were never in the demo. Most SEs are nowhere to be found by then. They thought the technical win was the finish line.

It was the qualifier.

The best SEs I've watched don't disengage after the POC. They treat the post-POC stretch as the real work. They clear security clearances before procurement asks and they give the AE a business case legal can actually defend.

I've been saying this for 2 years. Intelligence needs to be decoupled from records. And Headless 360 proves it. Once the layer of records gets commoditized, leverage moves to whoever builds the intelligence layer on top of it.

## ANTI-CONVERGENCE: PRIOR ARGUMENTS (do NOT repeat these theses)
- The 5% of enterprise AI that works wins because it's vertical, context-rich, and embedded in existing workflows — not because the technology is better.
- Enterprise AI fails not because the technology is immature but because organizations treat AI adoption as a tech project instead of a context-delivery problem.
- Enterprise AI succeeds only when it widens the gap for top performers rather than trying to democratize mediocrity across the org.
- Enterprise AI projects fail because companies measure AI success by model accuracy instead of by whether the person closest to the problem can actually use the output in their next decision.
- Enterprise AI projects fail at scale because companies skip the 'prove it in one plant' phase — the 5% win by treating narrow, measurable wins as the foundation for expansion, not as small thinking.
- Enterprise AI fails not because companies pick the wrong technology but because they refuse to kill the internal tool after building it — the 5% that win are the ones who buy the workflow and only build the glue.

## STORIES ALREADY USED (do NOT reuse)
- Box down 27% but AI spend up 60%
- Figma S-1 analysis: $525M ARR, 70% revenue from large accounts, 40+ customers at $1M+ ARR

- Presales leader at dinner saying 'the SEs prepping hardest are losing the most deals'
- SiftHub revenue concentration and internal workflow validation practices
- The CRO who quit a large B2B startup after 8 months — context scattered across Slack, Notion, and old decks

## THE 13 PROVEN OPENER MECHANICS
1. Specific number + context — "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers."
3. First-person confession — "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."
9. Tension pair — "The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans."
10. Before/after snapshot — "Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three."
11. Micro story — "An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores."
12. Authority redirect — "McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed."
13. Data inversion — "95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers."
